topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet swam before my eyes. I’d been meticulously tracking project allocations for the last quarter, a task I usually found soothing in its order. Then I saw the final compensation breakdown for the department. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as my gaze landed on Sarah’s entry. Her salary, juxtaposed against mine, felt like a physical blow. I reread the figures three times, a faint tremor running through my hands. When Mark, the senior manager, called me into his office, the air felt thick and suffocating. I kept my voice level, too level, while staring at the polished mahogany desk. "I've reviewed the compensation structure," he began, his tone utterly devoid of warmth. I nodded, the movement stiff. The silence stretched, filled only by the frantic drumming in my own ears. When he finally dismissed me, I walked back to my desk, the fluorescent lights seeming too bright, and felt a profound sense of nausea rising in my throat.

[story 2]

It happened during the monthly review meeting. I was preparing to present my metrics, feeling a familiar, tight pressure behind my ribs. When the HR representative announced the tiered salary structure, the numbers flashed across the screen, and a sudden, sharp chill swept over me. I focused intently on the projector screen, trying to anchor myself in the data, but my attention kept snagging on the figure associated with Alex. It was a significant gap. I felt a sudden, hot flush creeping up my neck, and I gripped my pen so tightly my knuckles turned white. Later that afternoon, I found myself staring blankly at my monitor, unable to focus on anything else. Every time I glanced at Alex’s name, a sour taste coated my mouth. I made a conscious effort to breathe slowly, but the air felt thin, inadequate.

[story 3]

I found the data buried deep within an archived file, a clumsy attempt to justify my own workload. The realization hit me like a physical impact. The discrepancy was staggering. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. I wanted to slam the laptop shut, to retreat into the anonymity of my cubicle, but my feet remained rooted to the floor. The room seemed to shrink around me, the sounds of the office—the distant phone ringing, the hum of the ventilation—becoming amplified and grating. I spent the next hour meticulously re-reading the figures, my jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. When my supervisor approached, his casual greeting felt like an invasion. I managed a weak smile, but inside, everything felt heavy, weighted down by a sudden, unwelcome clarity.
